%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# First-Order Pole (or Zero)

> **Section Objective:** Learn how a first-order pole or zero at s = -a shapes Bode magnitude and phase plots.

## Concepts In This Section

- Log magnitude asymptote
- Corner frequency and error
- Phase asymptote
- Pole-zero mirror rule

## 1. Log Magnitude of a First-Order Pole

For a first-order pole at \(s = -a\), the Bode magnitude contribution follows a simple two-segment rule:

- **Below the corner:** When \(\omega \ll a\), the contribution is approximately **0 dB** — the pole has almost no effect.
- **Above the corner:** When \(\omega \gg a\), the contribution falls as a straight line with slope **-20 dB/decade**.

The two asymptotes meet at \(\omega = a\), called the **corner frequency** (also called the break frequency). This is the only frequency you need to locate before sketching.

#### Example

If \(a = 100\) rad/s, the bend occurs at 100 rad/s: flat to the left, dropping at -20 dB/decade to the right.

## 2. Phase of a First-Order Pole

This is the log magnitude contribution of one first-order pole at \(s = -a\).

**Symbols:** \(\omega\) = frequency in rad/s, \(a\) = positive corner frequency in rad/s, \(j\) = imaginary-axis unit.

**When to use it:** Whenever a transfer function contains a first-order pole factor with corner frequency \(a\) — equivalently, a denominator factor of the form \((1 + j\omega/a)\).

**Exam trigger:** A factor that behaves like \(1/(1 + j\omega/a)\) in the denominator.

**Most common misuse:** Drawing the -20 dB/decade slope *before* the corner frequency instead of *after* it. The slope only begins at \(\omega = a\).

$$-20\log\left|1 + j\frac{\omega}{a}\right|$$

$$-10\log\left(1 + \frac{\omega^2}{a^2}\right)$$
*This is the exact pole magnitude written without the absolute-value expression — it is algebraically identical to the previous formula.

**When to use it:** Use this form to check the actual curve value at any specific frequency, especially at the corner.

**Key reading rule:** At \(\omega = a\), the exact value is **3 dB below** the asymptotic corner value. This is the maximum approximation error.

**Warning:** This exact formula is *not* the asymptote. The asymptote is the quick two-segment straight-line sketch (0 dB then -20 dB/decade). Use this formula only when you need to verify or plot the true curve.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## 2. Phase of a First-Order Pole

The phase contribution of a first-order pole at \(s = -a\) transitions smoothly from 0 degrees to -90 degrees — but this transition is **spread over two decades**, not concentrated at one frequency.

### THREE-LINE ASYMPTOTE RULE

| Frequency range | Phase asymptote |
|---|---|
| \(\omega \leq a/10\) | 0 degrees (flat) |
| \(a/10 < \omega < 10a\) | Straight line, slope -45 degrees/decade |
| \(\omega \geq 10a\) | -90 degrees (flat) |

#### Example

If \(a = 100\) rad/s, the sloping phase segment runs from 10 rad/s to 1000 rad/s.

### COMMON MISTAKE

The phase transition is **not** a sharp step at \(\omega = a\). It is a gradual slope spanning two full decades. Drawing an instant jump at the corner frequency is the most common phase-sketch error.

$$\angle H(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{a}\right)$$
*This is the exact phase contribution of one first-order pole at \(s = -a\).

**Symbols:** \(\angle H(j\omega)\) = phase in degrees (when arctangent is evaluated in degrees), \(\omega\) = frequency in rad/s, \(a\) = corner frequency in rad/s.

**When to use it:** When plotting or numerically checking the phase of a first-order pole at any specific frequency.

**Exam trigger:** A first-order denominator factor with corner frequency \(a\) — i.e., a factor of the form \((1 + j\omega/a)\) in the denominator.

**Most common misuse:** Drawing the phase as an instant jump from 0 degrees to -90 degrees exactly at \(\omega = a\). The exact formula shows the transition is gradual, and the three-line asymptote approximates it over the range \(a/10\) to \(10a\).*

$$\text{zero contribution} = -\,(\text{pole contribution})$$
*A first-order zero at \(s = -a\) has the same magnitude and phase shapes as the corresponding pole, but with the **sign reversed**.

- **Magnitude:** The zero magnitude is the mirror image of the pole magnitude across the 0 dB line. Slope after the corner is **+20 dB/decade** (upward).
- **Phase:** The zero phase is the mirror image of the pole phase across the 0 degree line. Phase goes from 0 degrees up toward **+90 degrees**.

**Exam trigger:** A numerator factor of the form \((1 + j\omega/a)\) instead of a denominator factor.

**Most common misuse:** Using the correct corner frequency \(\omega = a\) but forgetting to flip the sign — drawing a -20 dB/decade slope or a downward phase for a zero.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="comparison_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- **Corner frequency:** The bend in both magnitude and phase occurs at \(\omega = a\).
- **Pole log magnitude:** \(-20\log\left|1 + j\dfrac{\omega}{a}\right|\) — flat at 0 dB before \(a\), then -20 dB/decade after \(a\).
- **Exact pole magnitude:** \(-10\log\!\left(1 + \dfrac{\omega^2}{a^2}\right)\) — use this to check the true curve value.
- **Maximum magnitude error:** 3 dB at \(\omega = a\); about 1 dB one octave away; about 0.3 dB two octaves away.
- **Pole phase:** \(\angle H(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{a}\right)\) — transitions from 0 degrees to -90 degrees.
- **Phase asymptote rule:** 0 degrees for \(\omega \leq a/10\); straight line at -45 degrees/decade from \(a/10\) to \(10a\); -90 degrees for \(\omega \geq 10a\).
- **Pole magnitude slope after corner:** -20 dB/decade. **Zero magnitude slope after corner:** +20 dB/decade.
- **Pole-zero mirror rule:** zero contribution \(= -\)(pole contribution) — mirror across 0 dB for magnitude, mirror across 0 degrees for phase.
- **Exam trap:** Never draw an instant phase jump at \(\omega = a\); the transition spans two decades.

*Next, we will extend these sketching rules to second-order poles and zeros.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-quiz-plan" data-quiz-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
